ORDER DENYING PETITIONER'S PETITION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE
AMENDED PETITION FOR WRIT OF ADMINISTRATIVE MANDAMUS FILED BY PETITIONER RETIREMENT BOARD OF THE SAN FRANCISCO CITY AND COUNTY EMPLOYEES'  RETIREMENT SYSTEM

LAW AND MOTION 302, PETITIONER RETIREMENT BOARD OF THE SAN FRANCISCO CITY AND COUNTY EMPLOYEES' RETIREMENT SYSTEM'S WRIT OF MANDATE. ARGUED AND THE COURT ADOPTS AND MODIFIES ITS TENTATIVE RULING: THE WRIT IS DENIED. THE PARTIES AGREE THAT THE APPLICABLE PROVISIONS ARE SET FORTH IN THE NOVEMBER 7, 2000 CHARTER AMENDMENT, SECTION A.8-587-9. THE REASONABLE INTERPRETATION OF THAT SECTION AND THE PLAN AS A WHOLE IS THAT THE DISQUALIFICATION LANGUAGE IS APPLICABLE ONLY TO SERVICE RETIREMENT. THE FACT THAT THE DISQUALIFICATION LANGUAGE IS NOT INCLUDED IN THE INCAPACITY SECTION AND/OR IS NOT SET FORTH IN A STAND ALONE SECTION SUPPORTS THIS INTERPRETATION. IN THE COURT'S VIEW, THE CITY'S RELIANCE ON THE 1966 VOTER INITIATIVE SUPPORTS, RATHER THAN UNDERMINES, THE INTERPRETATION BECAUSE THE SUBSEQUENT AMENDMENTS DO NOT INCLUDE (FOR WHATEVER REASON) THE BLANKET AND UNEQUIVOCAL DISQUALIFICATION FOR ALL FORMS OF RETIREMENT. ALTHOUGH THE RETIREMENT BOARD'S POSITION IS UNDERSTANDABLE AND CONSISTENT WITH THE 1966 VOTER INITIATIVE, IT APPEARS THAT THIS IS THE FIRST CASE WHERE A PERSON HAS HAD OCCASION TO DENY A CLAIM FOR DISABILITY OR VESTING BENEFITS WHERE THE APPLICANT HAS BEEN CONVICTED OF MORAL TURPITUDE. AS A CONSEQUENCE, THE BOARD'S DECISION IS NOT ENTITLED TO PERSUASIVE WEIGHT. RESPONDENT'S OTHER ARGUMENTS FOR AFFIRMANCE OF THE DECISION BY THE ADMINISTRATIVE LAW JUDGE ARE UNPERSUASIVE. COUNSEL STIPULATE THAT THE ADMINISTRATIVE RECORD (FILED BY THE CLERK'S OFFICE ON JUNE 1, 2007) MAY BE MADE A PART OF THE FILE. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. JUDGE: PATRICK J. MAHONEY, REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO, CSR #8600

calendar.matters:
Notice Of Hearing On Petition For Writ Of Mandate ; Request For Judicial Notice; Joint Administrative Record;
Off Calendar Jul-31-2007 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: PETITIONER RETIREMENT BOARD OF THE SAN FRANCISCO CITY AND COUNTY EMPOYEES' RETIREMENT SYSTEM'S:
APPEARANCES: MARTIN R. GRAN, DEPUTY CITY ATTORNEY (OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY, 415.554.4291) APPEARING FOR PETITIONER. RICHARD K. GROSBOLL, ESQUIRE (NEYHART ANDERSON FLYNN & GROSBOLL, 415.677.9440) APPEARING FOR THE REAL-PARTY-IN-INTEREST.
RULING: ARGUED AND THE COURT ADOPTS AND MODIFIES ITS TENTATIVE RULING: Petitioner Retiremant Board of San Francisco's Writ of Mandate. The writ is denied. The parties agree that the applicable provisions are set forth in the November 7, 2000 Charter amendment, section A.8-587-9. The reasonable interpretation of that section and the Plan as a whole is that the disqualification language is applicable only to service retirement. The fact that the disqualification language is not included in the incapacity section and/or is not set forth in a stand alone section supports this interpretation. In the Court's view, the City's reliance on the 1966 voter initiative supports, rather than undermines, the interpretation because the subsequent amendments do not include (for whatever reason) the blanket and unequivocal disqualification for all forms of retirement. Although the Retirement Board's position is understandable and consistent with the 1966 voter initiative, it appears that this is the first case where a person has had occasion to deny a claim for disability or vesting benefits where the applicant has been convicted of moral turpitude. As a consequence, the Board's decision is not entitled to persuasive weight. Respondent's other arguments for affirmance of the decision by the Administrative Law Judge are unpersuasive. COUNSEL STIPULATE THAT THE ADMINISTRATIVE RECORD (FILED BY THE CLERK'S OFFICE ON JUNE 1, 2007) MAY BE MADE A PART OF THE FILE. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. (302/PJM) JUDGE: PATRICK J. MAHONEY, REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO, CSR 8600
